title: rootless: detect and remove stale pause.pid with recycled PIDs
body:
When a pause process dies and its PID gets recycled by an unrelated process, the stale pause.pid file causes join failures.  Detect this by checking /proc/<pid>/environ for _PODMAN_PAUSE=1 after a failed join attempt.  If the process is not a pause process, remove the stale file and log a warning so the caller can create a fresh pause process on the first try.

Closes: https://github.com/containers/podman/issues/28157

comments.body:
> The namespace creation post boot (i.e. without alive lock held) is unlocked I belive, does check if valid and deleting the file not open us to rare race conditions.
> 
> process A checks if file exists process B checks if file exists process A removes file
> 
> process C has no pause pid file and creates it Process B removes the file
> 
> Process C now has a stale namespace
> 
> Just a theory, I am not sure this would actually cause real problems. I guess if there is a such a race then this would not really make it worse as currently the user would need to delete the file manually.
> 
> On thing I would like to see though is a test case in test/system/550-pause-process.bats because the reexec logic is quite fragile. We already kill the pause process in tests there, I think we could kill + create some sleep process and write its pid to the pause pid file and check that this recovers from that scenario with the warning message automatically.

sure, in theory that can happen but the assumption is that it is such an exceptional error, that we won't hit that race condition.

I don't want to overengineer this with locks, so alternatively we could just print an error asking users to delete that file and don't have to deal with it directly.

> I don't want to overengineer this with locks, so alternatively we could just print an error asking users to delete that file and don't have to deal with it directly.

Yeah like I said the alternative is we move this to the user which does not solve the race so we do not make anything worse as such I agree it is fine to not introduce more locks. And I agree it will be so exceptional that I am not sure we would ever hit this in the wild so not worth trying to spend to much time on it.

I guess overall this is just another argument for your new ns refernce code path on newer kernels where we no longer track pids.
